subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

reviews.author: Namhyung Kim
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Namhyung Kim pointed out that the patch description does not clearly explain why the current fixup is wrong by construction and requested a more detailed explanation of the problem.

Reviewer Namhyung Kim requested a rebase of the patch series onto the latest perf-tools-next branch due to inapplicability and also asked for removal of an empty line in the code.
reviews.raw_body:
Hello,

On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 07:17:00AM +0000, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:

---

Can you please remove the blank line here?

Also, this series cannot apply anymore.  Please rebase onto the latest
perf-tools-next.

Thanks,
Namhyung

2026-07-04.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in perf record where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are written to the file when the compressed output exceeds the maximum record size, leading to mis-framed data that cannot be decompressed. The fix involves changing how the header is set for each record, ensuring that it accurately reflects the compressed data size and aligns properly with 8-byte boundaries. This change also reduces the maximum record size by the size of a u64 to prevent overflows in the header.size field.

reviews.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged the need to address an issue where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are emitted per push when zstd compression is enabled, agreed to fix it in a rebased version of the patch series.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks for taking a look, Namhyung.

Absolutely. I'll fix it up, rebase on latest perf-tools-next and respin
the series.

2026-07-06.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in perf record where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are written to the file when the compressed output exceeds the maximum record size, leading to mis-framed data that cannot be decompressed. The fix involves changing how the header is set for each record, ensuring that it accurately reflects the compressed data size and aligns properly with 8-byte boundaries. This change also reduces the maximum record size by the size of a u64 to prevent overflows in the header.size field.
